content: The 2012 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Robert J. Lefkowitz and Brian K. Kobilka for their work on G-protein coupled receptors, which are crucial for cellular signaling.
raw_content: The 2012 Nobel Prize in Chemistry was awarded to Robert J. Lefkowitz and Brian K. Kobilka for their work on G-protein coupled receptors, which are crucial for cellular signaling.
reasoning:

Maybe I should think about the areas that were prominent in 2012. I think there was some focus on molecular biology or cellular processes around that time.

Wait, I think I remember hearing about something related to G-protein coupled receptors. Those are important in cell signaling, right? So maybe someone who worked on that won the prize. I think the name Robert Lefkowitz comes to mind. He's an American scientist, if I'm not mistaken. And there was another person who worked with him, perhaps someone named Brian Kobilka. I believe they were awarded for their studies on G-protein coupled receptors, which are crucial for how cells respond to external signals.

Let me try to piece this together. The Nobel Prize in Chemistry for 2012 was awarded to Robert J. Lefkowitz and Brian K. Kobilka for their work on G-protein coupled receptors. That sounds right because I remember reading about their research in scientific breakthroughs around that year. They were both from the United States, I think. Lefkowitz was at Duke University, and Kobilka was at Stanford University. Their discoveries helped in understanding how cells communicate, which has implications for drug development.

I'm pretty sure that's correct, but to be thorough, I should consider if there were any other notable chemists around that time. For example, in 2011, the prize was awarded for the discovery of quasicrystals, and in 2013, it was about the development of computational models. So 2012 being about GPCRs fits the pattern of recognizing significant advancements in cellular biology and chemistry.

So, putting it all together, the winners were Robert J. Lefkowitz and Brian K. Kobilka for their work on G-protein coupled receptors. I don't think there were any other winners that year, so it must have been just the two of them sharing the prize.
